title: The Relation Between the Geodesic Flow and Finite-Area Holomorphic Quadratic Differentials on Infinite(-genus) Riemann Surfaces
speaker: Dragomir Saric
abstract: The Hopf-Tsuji-Sullivan theorem states that the geodesic flow on (an infinite) Riemann surface is ergodic iff the Poincare series is divergent iff the Brownian motion is recurrent. Infinite Riemann surfaces can be built by gluing infinitely many hyperbolic pairs of pants, and the Riemann surface is determined by the corresponding Fenchel-Nielsen (FN) parameters. We provide various sufficient conditions on FN parameters to guarantee that the surface belongs to this class (joint with Hakobyan and Basmajian). Then we prove the geodesic flow is ergodic iff a.e. horizontal leaf of every finite-area holomorphic quadratic differential is recurrent. We use this criterion to decide when the geodesic flow is not ergodic in terms of the FN parameters.

proof_stages.label: Infinite-type surfaces and the ergodic/parabolic class
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up hyperbolic Riemann surfaces X=H/Gamma with non-finitely-generated Gamma and recalls the Hopf-Tsuji-Sullivan equivalences between ergodic geodesic flow, divergence of the Poincare series, recurrent Brownian motion/no Green's function, and infinite extremal distance to infinity.

proof_stages.label: Extremal distance as the usable criterion
proof_stages.summary: Modulus of curve families is defined via admissible conformal metrics, its conformal and quasiconformal invariance is recalled, and the condition of infinite extremal distance from a compact core to the boundary at infinity is singled out as the most checkable form of the Hopf-Tsuji-Sullivan criterion.

proof_stages.label: Fenchel-Nielsen data and standard-collar bounds
proof_stages.summary: Lengths and twists of a pants decomposition are introduced as the relevant geometric data, and summing extremal distances across disjoint standard collars yields the sufficient divergence condition sum exp(-ell_n/2)/ell_n=infinity while exposing a gap in the zero-twist case.

proof_stages.label: Nonstandard collars and twist-refined ergodicity criteria
proof_stages.summary: By enlarging collars with nonstandard slivers one removes the extraneous division by length, obtaining the Basmajian-Hakobyan-Saric criterion sum exp(-ell_n/2)=infinity for no Green's function/ergodic flow, together with a refinement incorporating normalized twists.

proof_stages.label: Cantor-tree rate gap and need for a converse method
proof_stages.summary: The collar criterion is applied to Cantor tree surfaces, then compared with McMullen's bounded-length nonergodicity result, leaving an intermediate-rate gap and motivating holomorphic quadratic differentials as a way to prove that a surface is not in the no-Green/ergodic class.

proof_stages.label: Infinite-surface Hubbard-Masur realization
proof_stages.summary: After recalling the compact Hubbard-Masur theorem, proper integrable partial measured foliations are defined on arbitrary Riemann surfaces and are stated to be uniquely realized by horizontal foliations of finite-area holomorphic quadratic differentials.

proof_stages.label: Recurrence criterion for finite-area quadratic differentials
proof_stages.summary: The central relation is stated: X has ergodic geodesic flow if and only if almost every horizontal leaf of every finite-area holomorphic quadratic differential on X is recurrent.

proof_stages.label: Applications via transient foliations and density results
proof_stages.summary: Using the contrapositive of the recurrence criterion, transient finite-integral foliations give nonergodicity for Cantor tree surfaces with faster level-length growth, and the same quadratic-differential viewpoint yields density of one-cylinder finite-area differentials in the no-Green class plus related results and questions on second-kind groups and quasiconformal invariance of potential-theoretic classes.

examples.engagement_reason: for Cantor tree-type surfaces the speaker sets levelwise geodesic lengths and applies the criteria and foliation construction to obtain ergodic versus Green-function-supporting regimes

examples.name: Zero-twist flute chain
examples.note: The zero-twist chain reveals the gap between the standard-collar criterion with an extra length denominator and the first-kind condition, motivating the nonstandard-collar improvement.
examples.engagement_reason: for the zero-twist flute chain the speaker traces the orthogonal concatenations, estimates distances as roughly exp(-length/2), and derives the associated summation/parabolicity criterion

passes.reason: The zero-twist flute chain is a specially aligned gluing in which orthogeodesics concatenate, giving a symmetric solvable case rather than a counterexample.

examples.name: Half-twist flute with logarithmic lengths
examples.note: Taking every twist to be one half and comparing lengths such as 2 log n and 4 log n illustrates how twist information strengthens the sufficient ergodicity criterion.
